track_listing.track: Son Of Mirror Man - Mere Man

transcript.text: On 275 and 285. Also broadcasting in stereo on VHF. This is National Radio 1.
Well, hello there. Good evening to you. This is TV on the Radio, Thomas Vance. Welcome once again to the Friday Rock Show.
And tonight, the second in our flashback series. The whole of this program is going to be devoted to 1968.
You'll hear some rare sessions by Family, Jethro Tull and Status Quo, and a really rare one by Fleetwood Mac.
Also, we'll be playing tracks by the Rolling Stones, Captain Beefheart, Chicken Shack, Hendrix, Floyd, Dylan, and Jeff Beck.
Now, 1968 was not a head-banging year, so don't expect any heavy metal,
but you will hear the first-ever single by Deep Purple and loads and loads of classics.
So if you're interested, stay tuned.
We start with this.
I'm the God of Hellfire, and I bring you fire.
Flashback 68, that was Judy Driscoll, Brian Auger, and the Trinity,
and a song that got to number five in April 1968 is a single written by Bob Dylan,
and it was called This Wheel Is On Fire.

or the launch, I think it's called, up there in Coventry.
And before that, you heard something that got to number one in June 1968
by The Crazy World of Arthur Brown.
It was called Fire, co-written by Vincent Crane,
who went on to form Atomic Rooster after that.
And also, did you know that Karl Palmer was in the band as well?
And he, of course, has got a new band at the moment called P.M.
Their album is out. It's called 1 P.M.

who wanted me to say hello to his love, Teresa.

And they live in Fife.

It was on the second LP put out by Captain Beefheart and his magic band.
The album was called Strictly Personal.
And the track was Son of Mirror Man, Mere Man.
And before that something from the 1968 album that came out in round about November or December of that year by the Rolling Stones.
The album was called Beggar's Banquet.
The track you heard was Street Fighting Man.

at No. 5 Rippon Square, Felgate, Jarrow and Tyne and Weir.

will remember that it was delayed for five months

but ultimately they put it out.
And now let's go to our first archive session from 1968, Family.
